summary: Microsoft documents HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins as the Defender policy that hides exclusions from local admins. In Win25H2Clean, a managed exclusion stayed present on the policy exclusions branch, but Get-MpPreference stopped showing it when the documented root policy path was set to 1. Current 25H2 builds also honor a Policy Manager alias for the same behavior.

setting.name: Hide Defender Exclusions From Local Admins
setting.casual_explanation: Defender can keep managed exclusions in effect but stop local admins from seeing them in the usual Defender admin surfaces.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change this when they want managed exclusions to stay enforced without exposing the exclusion list to local administrators.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: This does not change scan enforcement. It changes who can inspect exclusions through local admin tools.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins on the Defender policy surface with 0 or not configured meaning local admins can view exclusions and 1 meaning they cannot. In the clean 25H2 VM, a managed exclusion under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions\Paths was visible in Get-MpPreference when the policy was unset. After writing HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1 on the root policy path, wmiprvse.exe and powershell.exe both read that value and Get-MpPreference returned 'N/A: Administrators are not allowed to view exclusions'. A second run showed the same behavior when only HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Policy Manager\HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins was set to 1, so current 25H2 builds honor both surfaces.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the policy unset so local admins can view managed exclusions.

allowed_values.label: Exclusions visible to local admins
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and local admins can inspect managed exclusions through the usual Defender surfaces.

allowed_values.label: Hide managed exclusions from local admins
allowed_values.meaning: Managed exclusions stay on the policy exclusions branch, but Get-MpPreference hides them from local admin views.

targets.notes: The official path is the root Defender policy key. Win25H2Clean also honored a Policy Manager alias with the same value name, but the app stays on the documented root path.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1 on the documented root Defender policy path.

windows_defaults.label: Policy unset baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where the Defender exclusion-visibility policy is not configured

states.rationale: Leave the policy unset so local admins can see managed exclusions.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where local admins should still be able to inspect managed Defender exclusions
avoid_for: Locked-down managed systems that intentionally hide exclusion lists from local admins

states.rationale: Keep the exclusion-visibility policy unset until there is a reason to hide the managed list.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the normal local-admin inspection behavior for managed exclusions.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need managed exclusions enforced but hidden from local admins.
tradeoffs: Does not hide the managed exclusion list from local admin tools.

recommended_profiles.label: Hide managed exclusions from local admins
avoid_for: Systems that expect local admins to audit managed Defender exclusions

states.rationale: Use the documented root Defender policy path that wmiprvse.exe and powershell.exe read directly in the 25H2 VM.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want managed exclusions to stay active while local admins lose the normal exclusion listing views.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the most transparent local admin inspection path.
tradeoffs: Get-MpPreference stops listing the managed exclusion even though it still exists on the managed policy branch.
Current 25H2 builds also honor a Policy Manager alias, but the app uses the documented root path.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Defender CSP HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins with 0 or not configured meaning exclusions stay visible to local admins and 1 meaning local admins can no longer view exclusions.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Configure exclusions in Defender Antivirus
evidence.summary: Microsoft notes that HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins affects whether local admins can view exclusions and that the setting does not remove exclusions from policy storage.

evidence.title: Windows Defender dump list includes root and Policy Manager HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins
evidence.summary: The local Defender dump list shows HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins under both HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender and HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Policy Manager.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean baseline visibility with managed exclusion present
evidence.summary: With both HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins surfaces unset, a managed exclusion under the policy exclusions branch still appeared in Get-MpPreference and remained present in the managed policy hive.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean root-path read for HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1
evidence.summary: After writing HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1 on the root Defender policy path, wmiprvse.exe and powershell.exe both queried HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins and read Data: 1.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean visibility change with root-path HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1
evidence.summary: With the root policy path set to 1, the managed exclusion still existed on the policy exclusions branch, but Get-MpPreference returned 'N/A: Administrators are not allowed to view exclusions'.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Policy Manager alias for HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1
evidence.summary: With only the Policy Manager surface set to 1, wmiprvse.exe first saw the root value as NAME NOT FOUND and then read HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Policy Manager\HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1. The paired visibility JSON showed the same hidden-exclusions behavior.

evidence.title: Current security provider HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins write
evidence.summary: The app writes HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins = 1 on the documented root path.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the value model clearly, and the Win25H2Clean VM shows the documented root policy path hiding managed exclusions from Get-MpPreference while the managed exclusions branch stays populated. The Policy Manager alias is a current-build side observation, not a blocker for the documented root path.

open_questions: Should the Policy Manager alias be modeled as a separate audit record or folded into this record permanently as a current-build alias?
Should the app stay on the documented root path only, or is there any Defender scenario where writing both surfaces is required?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: wmiprvse.exe | RegQueryValue | HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\HideExclusionsFromLocalAdmins | SUCCESS | Type: REG_DWORD, Length: 4, Data: 1
validation_proof.notes: The baseline visibility JSON showed the managed exclusion path in Get-MpPreference while both policy values were unset. The root-path state-1 run then hid that exclusion from Get-MpPreference, and a separate Policy Manager run produced the same behavior after reading the alias path instead.
